diagrams.title: Singly Linked List Structure
diagrams.description: A visual representation of a singly linked list showing nodes arranged horizontally. Each node contains a data field and a next pointer (arrow) pointing to the subsequent node. The last node's next pointer points to null (represented as NULL or Ø). The head pointer indicates the first node. This diagram helps students understand the basic unidirectional traversal characteristic of singly linked lists.
diagrams.purpose: Explains the fundamental structure of a singly linked list where each node only knows about the next node, enabling only forward traversal from head to tail.

diagrams.title: Doubly Linked List Structure
diagrams.description: A visual representation of a doubly linked list showing nodes with three distinct sections: prev pointer (left arrow), data field (center), and next pointer (right arrow). Arrows point in both directions to demonstrate bidirectional traversal. Both head's prev and tail's next point to null. This diagram illustrates how doubly linked lists allow traversal in both forward and backward directions.
elements: node boxes split into three sections
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the bidirectional nature of doubly linked lists where each node maintains references to both its predecessor and successor, enabling reverse traversal.

diagrams.title: Circular Linked Lists (Singly and Doubly)
diagrams.description: A comparison diagram showing both singly circular and doubly circular linked lists side by side. The singly circular variant shows nodes connected in a ring where the last node's next points back to head (no null). The doubly circular variant shows the same ring structure but with additional prev pointers forming a complete bidirectional circle. This visualization helps understand how circular lists eliminate the null endpoints and can traverse indefinitely.
elements: singly circular: unidirectional arrows in circle
doubly circular: bidirectional arrows in circle
diagrams.purpose: Explains circular linked list variants and how they differ from linear lists by having the last node point back to the first, useful for round-robin scheduling and cyclical data scenarios.
